narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 61.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in earlier years of schooling, where your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile—meaning you ranked near the bottom of your class—compared to the average student's 5th or 6th decile position. That lower Year 3 standing was the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance through law school and the bar. Your Year 1 grades, in the 3rd decile and also below the average of around the 6th decile, reinforced this concern as the third-most influential element, further tilting the odds against passing. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 35.5 was the second-most important factor and worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success despite being slightly below the average of 36.7, showing your aptitude for the kind of reasoning the bar demands. Your middle family income bracket and plan to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students, played lesser roles and didn't sway the outcome much either way. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile stands out for those weaker early decile rankings, which often correlate with struggles on the high-stakes exam, even with your respectable GPA and LSAT. While this rejection stings, especially given your background and preparation, it doesn't define your potential—many students in similar positions improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking tutoring to boost your class rankings, perhaps aiming for summer programs that strengthen foundational skills, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends in your academics. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or bar passage support programs tailored to full-time students like yourself. With persistence, you can reshape this story and position yourself for success on the bar.
